past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 6 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Hypothyroidism, diagnosed 10 years ago, on stable replacement therapy
Mild depression, managed with SSRI for the past 3 years
family_history: Father died of lung cancer at age 68
Mother alive with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Brother with no significant medical history
No family history of brain tumors or neurological disease

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 2-3 times per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and teenage daughter in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Previously active, regular walking and yoga; stopped 6 weeks ago due to fatigue

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Appears mildly anxious. Speech is fluent but notable for intermittent word-finding pauses, particularly for low-frequency nouns. She uses circumlocutions (e.g., 'the thing you write with' for pen). Comprehension is intact for simple and moderately complex commands. Repetition is intact. Reading is intact. Writing shows occasional paraphasic errors. Attention is mildly impaired — she makes two errors on serial sevens. Memory is 3/3 on immediate recall, 2/3 on delayed recall at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally; however, there is a questionable subtle right upper quadrant visual field deficit on repeat testing that the patient reports is inconsistent. Extraocular movements are full without nystagmus. No ptosis. Facial sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Mild right lower facial asymmetry noted, which the patient and husband report is new over the past few weeks. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 5/5 strength in deltoid, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, and finger abductors. Right lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: 5/5 strength throughout. Left lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy. Tone is normal throughout.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact bilaterally in all extremities. Pinprick intact bilaterally. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation. Graphesthesia and stereognosis intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally. Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal stride length and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No papilledema on fundoscopic examination. No meningismus. The word-finding difficulty is the most prominent exam finding and is subtle enough that a casual examiner might attribute it to anxiety.

patient.chief_complaint: Two months of progressive word-finding difficulty and personality change
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Deborah Marston is a 52-year-old right-handed woman and high school English teacher who was referred to the neurology service by her primary care physician after two months of progressive word-finding difficulty and personality changes noted by her family. Her husband reports that the first noticeable change was approximately two months ago when she began misplacing common words during dinner conversations — something highly unusual for a woman who has taught literature for 24 years and has always been exceptionally articulate. Initially, the family attributed this to stress and fatigue related to the school year. Over the following weeks, however, the word-finding difficulty became more prominent and was accompanied by a subtle but unmistakable change in her personality. Her husband describes her as becoming increasingly apathetic and emotionally flat, quite unlike her previously warm and expressive demeanor. She stopped initiating plans with friends, which was out of character, and on several occasions became unusually irritable over trivial matters. Approximately six weeks ago, she began experiencing headaches that she described as a dull, persistent pressure predominantly on the left side, worse in the morning upon waking and sometimes associated with mild nausea. She attributed these to tension headaches and increased her use of over-the-counter ibuprofen without significant relief. Three weeks ago, her daughter noticed that she struggled to help with a crossword puzzle, something the two of them had enjoyed together for years, and that she seemed to stare blankly for brief moments. There has been no witnessed seizure activity, though the family is uncertain whether these brief staring episodes could represent seizures. She denies any visual changes, though on directed questioning she admits that she occasionally has difficulty seeing things on her right side when driving, leading her to stop driving two weeks ago. She has lost approximately 4 kg of weight over the past two months without intentional dieting, which she attributes to reduced appetite. Her primary care physician ordered an outpatient MRI that revealed a left temporal lobe lesion, prompting urgent referral and admission.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric but occasionally disrupted over the left temporal region
background.sleep_architecture: Not assessed during this 30-minute routine recording

findings.location: Left temporal region (T3-T5, extending to F7)
findings.clinical_correlation: Suggestive of underlying structural pathology in the left temporal region

findings.frequency: Reduced amplitude and slightly slower background over the left temporal-parietal region compared to the right
findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with structural lesion affecting the left hemisphere

artifacts.severity: Mild, intermittent, not significantly limiting interpretation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Mildly accentuates the left temporal slowing

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to intermittent left temporal polymorphic delta activity and mild left hemispheric background asymmetry. No definite epileptiform discharges were identified during this recording. The regional slowing is suggestive of an underlying structural lesion in the left temporal region. Clinical correlation with neuroimaging is recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording; no sleep was captured. Interictal epileptiform discharges may not be captured on a single routine EEG. Prolonged or sleep-deprived EEG may be warranted if seizures are suspected clinically.
recommended_actions: Consider prolonged or ambulatory EEG if clinical seizures are suspected
Consider anti-seizure prophylaxis in the peri-operative setting

findings.location: Left temporal lobe, predominantly involving the superior and middle temporal gyri, with extension into the insular cortex

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense to isointense relative to gray matter
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous hyperintense signal with areas of mixed signal centrally
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with surrounding vasogenic edema extending into the temporal white matter and external capsule
signal_characteristics.DWI: Mild restricted diffusion at portions of the enhancing margin, suggesting high cellularity
signal_characteristics.Post-contrast T1: Irregular, heterogeneous enhancement without classic ring pattern; patchy areas of non-enhancement centrally may represent early necrosis or low-grade components

findings.mass_effect: Mild effacement of the left temporal horn of the lateral ventricle. No significant midline shift (estimated 2 mm leftward falcine shift). Mild compression of the left sylvian fissure.
findings.borders: Irregular and poorly defined margins, particularly at the medial and superior aspects where the lesion blends into adjacent FLAIR-bright edema. Difficult to distinguish tumor margin from surrounding edema.

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified in the remainder of the brain
No evidence of hemorrhage or calcification within the lesion
Major intracranial vessels are patent on MRA sequences
Remainder of the brain parenchyma is unremarkable for age
mri.impression: 1. Irregular heterogeneously enhancing left temporal mass measuring 3.8 x 2.9 x 3.1 cm with surrounding vasogenic edema and mild mass effect. The enhancement pattern is atypical — not the classic ring enhancement of glioblastoma, but the infiltrative borders and restricted diffusion at the enhancing margin raise concern for a high-grade glial neoplasm. 2. The lesion involves the superior and middle temporal gyri with extension toward the insular cortex, placing it in proximity to language-associated cortex. 3. No additional intracranial lesions identified to suggest metastatic disease. 4. Differential diagnosis includes high-grade glioma (glioblastoma or anaplastic astrocytoma), lower-grade glioma with early anaplastic transformation, CNS lymphoma, and less likely tumefactive demyelination.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Irregular enhancement, infiltrative borders, restricted diffusion at margin suggesting high cellularity, surrounding vasogenic edema, patient demographics
Can present as an enhancing mass with restricted diffusion; however, typically more homogeneous enhancement and periventricular location. Immunocompetent status makes this less likely.
Solitary enhancing lesion could represent a metastasis; however, infiltrative borders and involvement of multiple gyri are more typical of a primary glial tumor. No known primary malignancy.
Could present with an enhancing lesion in a middle-aged woman; however, the mass effect, irregular enhancement, and restricted diffusion at the margin are less typical. No prior history of MS.

recommended_actions: MR perfusion to assess relative cerebral blood volume in the enhancing component
MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite ratios (choline/NAA, lipid/lactate)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to exclude occult primary malignancy
Neurosurgical consultation for stereotactic biopsy or surgical planning
Functional MRI for pre-surgical language lateralization given left temporal location

Additional.clinical_significance: Within normal limits; mildly elevated LDH can sometimes be seen with high-grade tumors but is non-specific

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are largely unremarkable. CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and hepatic function are all within normal limits, providing a suitable pre-surgical baseline. Normal renal and hepatic function supports candidacy for chemotherapy if indicated. TSH is well-controlled on current replacement. CRP is borderline but non-specific. LDH is within normal limits. The absence of significant laboratory abnormalities is consistent with a primary CNS neoplasm rather than a systemic process.

findings.location: Left temporal enhancing mass, particularly at the medial and superior enhancing rim

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Maximum rCBV ratio 3.8 relative to contralateral normal-appearing white matter (values >1.75 suggest high-grade neoplasm)
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Elevated regional cerebral blood flow corresponding to areas of elevated rCBV
signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Elevated permeability in the enhancing regions, consistent with disruption of the blood-brain barrier

findings.location: Central non-enhancing portion of the left temporal mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Reduced rCBV in central non-enhancing areas, consistent with necrosis or low-grade tumor component

additional_observations: The perfusion pattern shows heterogeneity with a gradient of higher rCBV at the enhancing periphery and lower values centrally
Surrounding FLAIR-bright edema demonstrates normal to mildly elevated rCBV, which may represent infiltrative tumor versus vasogenic edema
No areas of elevated perfusion elsewhere in the brain
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates elevated rCBV (max ratio 3.8) in the enhancing component of the left temporal mass, strongly supporting a high-grade neoplasm. The central low-perfusion area is consistent with necrosis. The heterogeneous perfusion pattern with high peripheral and low central values is characteristic of glioblastoma. The mildly elevated perfusion in the peritumoral FLAIR-abnormal region raises concern for infiltrative tumor extending beyond the enhancing margins.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: rCBV >3.0, heterogeneous perfusion with high peripheral values, central necrosis
Lymphoma typically shows moderately elevated rCBV but usually lower than GBM; the heterogeneous pattern favors GBM

recommended_actions: Proceed with MR spectroscopy for additional metabolic characterization
Use perfusion hotspot to guide stereotactic biopsy target

findings.location: Left temporal enhancing mass (voxel placed over enhancing rim)

signal_characteristics.Choline: Markedly elevated choline peak (Cho/Cr ratio 3.2; normal <1.5)
signal_characteristics.NAA: Markedly reduced N-acetylaspartate (NAA/Cr ratio 0.6; normal >1.5), consistent with neuronal loss or displacement
signal_characteristics.Lipid_Lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks, consistent with necrosis and anaerobic metabolism
signal_characteristics.Cho_NAA_ratio: Cho/NAA ratio 5.3 (markedly elevated; values >2.0 suggest high-grade neoplasm)

findings.location: FLAIR-bright region adjacent to the enhancing margin (2 cm lateral to the enhancing rim)

additional_observations: The spectroscopic pattern of elevated choline, reduced NAA, and lipid/lactate peaks in the enhancing component is characteristic of a high-grade glial neoplasm
The peritumoral voxel showing borderline choline elevation and mild NAA reduction suggests possible tumor infiltration beyond the enhancing margin, which is typical of diffuse gliomas
No 2-hydroxyglutarate peak was identified, though single-voxel spectroscopy has limited sensitivity for this metabolite
output.impression: MR spectroscopy of the left temporal mass demonstrates a metabolite profile highly consistent with a high-grade glial neoplasm: markedly elevated choline (Cho/Cr 3.2), markedly reduced NAA (NAA/Cr 0.6), and prominent lipid/lactate peaks indicating necrosis. The Cho/NAA ratio of 5.3 strongly supports a high-grade tumor. Additionally, borderline abnormal spectroscopy in the peritumoral region suggests infiltrative tumor extension. These findings, combined with the MR perfusion data, are highly suspicious for glioblastoma (WHO grade 4).
recommended_actions: Tissue diagnosis via stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection is required for definitive grading and molecular profiling
Molecular testing should include IDH1/2, MGMT promoter methylation, 1p/19q codeletion, TERT promoter, and ATRX

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis with Contrast.value: Clear. No pulmonary nodules or masses. No lymphadenopathy.
No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal.
Homogeneous attenuation. No focal lesions. Normal size.
Normal. No masses or ductal dilation.
Bilateral normal size and enhancement. No masses or hydronephrosis. Simple 8 mm left renal cortical cyst.
Uterus and ovaries unremarkable. No pelvic masses or lymphadenopathy.
No suspicious osseous lesions. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine.

output.interpretation: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast demonstrates no evidence of an occult primary malignancy. No pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, or lymphadenopathy to suggest metastatic disease. An incidental 8 mm simple left renal cyst is noted, which is of no clinical significance. These findings support the diagnosis of a primary CNS neoplasm rather than a cerebral metastasis.

Neuropathology.value: High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with significant mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and focal areas of palisading necrosis

Neuropathology.clinical_significance: High proliferative index consistent with high-grade neoplasm

Molecular Pathology.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype status; confirmatory IDH1/2 sequencing pending
IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; associated with more aggressive behavior compared to IDH-mutant tumors
Favorable prognostic marker; predicts better response to temozolomide chemotherapy
Absence of codeletion is expected in astrocytic tumors; supports astrocytoma rather than oligodendroglioma lineage
Common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; supports the diagnosis
Retained expression is typical of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma
EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype GBM; supports the diagnosis and may have therapeutic implications
Classic molecular signature of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy of the left temporal mass reveals glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4. Key histological features include high mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis. Molecular profile: IDH-wildtype, MGMT promoter methylated (favorable for temozolomide response), TERT promoter mutated (C228T), EGFR amplified, +7/-10. No 1p/19q codeletion. ATRX expression retained. The integrated diagnosis per WHO 2021 classification is: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, CNS WHO grade 4. MGMT promoter methylation is a favorable prognostic finding and supports the use of temozolomide in the treatment protocol.
abnormal_values_summary: Histology: High-grade astrocytic neoplasm with significant mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation, and focal areas of palisading necrosis  - Consistent with glioblastoma, WHO grade 4
Ki-67 Proliferative Index: 35%  - High proliferative index consistent with high-grade neoplasm
TERT Promoter Mutation: Present (C228T)  - Common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; supports the diagnosis
EGFR Amplification: Present  - EGFR amplification is common in IDH-wildtype GBM; supports the diagnosis and may have therapeutic implications
Chromosome 7 gain / Chromosome 10 loss: +7/-10 present  - Classic molecular signature of IDH-wildtype glioblastoma

findings.location: Broca's area (left inferior frontal gyrus) and Wernicke's area (left posterior superior temporal gyrus)

signal_characteristics.Broca_area: Robust activation in the left inferior frontal gyrus (pars opercularis and triangularis), located approximately 2.5 cm superior and anterior to the nearest tumor margin
signal_characteristics.Wernicke_area: Activation in the left posterior superior temporal gyrus, posterior margin of activation zone is approximately 1.2 cm from the posterosuperior tumor margin
signal_characteristics.Supplementary_motor_area: Normal activation in the bilateral supplementary motor areas
signal_characteristics.Laterality_index: Left hemisphere dominant for language (laterality index 0.78)

signal_characteristics.Hand_motor: Bilateral hand motor activation in expected precentral gyrus locations. Left hand motor area (right precentral gyrus) is normal. Right hand motor area (left precentral gyrus) is located well superior to the tumor margin, approximately 3.0 cm distant.
signal_characteristics.Face_motor: Left face motor area is approximately 2.0 cm superior to the tumor's superior margin

additional_observations: Language mapping was performed with silent word generation and sentence completion paradigms
Motor mapping was performed with finger tapping and lip pursing paradigms
The patient cooperated well with the fMRI paradigms; head motion was within acceptable limits
The close proximity of Wernicke's area activation (1.2 cm) to the posterosuperior tumor margin indicates risk to language function with aggressive resection in that region
Intraoperative cortical mapping (awake craniotomy) should be considered to maximize safe resection
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates left hemisphere dominance for language. Broca's area is located at a safe distance (2.5 cm) from the tumor. However, Wernicke's area activation is in close proximity (1.2 cm) to the posterosuperior tumor margin, indicating that aggressive resection in that region carries risk of receptive language deficit. Motor cortex is at a safe distance from the tumor. An awake craniotomy with intraoperative language mapping is recommended to optimize the extent of resection while preserving language function.
recommended_actions: Awake craniotomy with intraoperative cortical stimulation mapping recommended
Plan surgical approach to maximize resection of enhancing tumor while preserving the 1.2 cm corridor to Wernicke's area
Consider diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) tractography of the arcuate fasciculus to further delineate language pathways

output.query: glioblastoma IDH-wildtype MGMT methylated standard treatment protocol Stupp regimen

results.title: Radiotherapy plus Concomitant and Adjuvant Temozolomide for Glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.key_finding: Addition of temozolomide to radiotherapy for newly diagnosed glioblastoma improved median survival from 12.1 to 14.6 months and 2-year survival from 10.4% to 26.5%. This 'Stupp protocol' became the standard of care.

results.title: Effects of radiotherapy with concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide versus radiotherapy alone on survival in glioblastoma: 5-year analysis (EORTC-NCIC)
results.authors: Stupp R, Hegi ME, Mason WP, et al.
results.key_finding: Long-term follow-up confirmed durable benefit of combined temozolomide-radiotherapy. MGMT promoter methylation was the strongest predictor of benefit from temozolomide (5-year OS 13.8% vs 5.2% in unmethylated tumors).

results.title: A randomized trial of bevacizumab for newly diagnosed glioblastoma (RTOG 0825)
results.authors: Gilbert MR, Dignam JJ, Armstrong TS, et al.
results.key_finding: Adding bevacizumab to standard temozolomide-radiotherapy did not improve overall survival in newly diagnosed GBM but increased progression-free survival and was associated with increased symptom burden.

results.title: Tumor treating fields (TTFields) plus temozolomide for newly diagnosed glioblastoma (EF-14 trial)
results.authors: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al.
results.key_finding: TTFields added to maintenance temozolomide improved median OS to 20.9 months vs 16.0 months. Benefit was seen across subgroups including MGMT methylated tumors.

output.summary: The standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, remains the Stupp protocol: maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent radiation therapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide (75 mg/m2), then adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m2, days 1-5 of 28-day cycles for 6-12 cycles). MGMT promoter methylation is the strongest predictor of temozolomide benefit. Tumor treating fields (TTFields) are an FDA-approved adjunct that improves survival when added to maintenance temozolomide. Bevacizumab does not improve overall survival in the upfront setting. For this patient with MGMT-methylated, IDH-wildtype GBM, the prognosis is relatively favorable within the GBM population, with expected median survival of approximately 18-24 months with standard treatment.

output.proposed: Temozolomide 75 mg/m2 daily during radiation, then 150-200 mg/m2 days 1-5 every 28 days
interactions: Sertraline: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. May continue current SSRI therapy.
Levothyroxine: No direct interaction with temozolomide. Monitor TSH periodically as illness and treatment may alter thyroid requirements.
Lisinopril: No significant interaction. Monitor blood pressure as clinical status changes.
contraindications: Temozolomide is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dacarbazine (same class). Patient has no known allergy to dacarbazine.
Requires adequate hematologic function before each cycle (ANC >1500/uL, platelets >100,000/uL). Current counts are adequate.
warnings: Myelosuppression is the dose-limiting toxicity; complete blood counts must be monitored weekly during concurrent radiotherapy and before each adjuvant cycle

Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is required during concurrent chemoradiation — however, patient is allergic to penicillin (not sulfonamides; verify tolerance). If sulfonamide allergy is also present, consider pentamidine inhalation or dapsone as alternatives
Antiemetic prophylaxis recommended (ondansetron 8 mg prior to each dose)
Hepatotoxicity is rare but liver function should be monitored
Avoid ibuprofen or other NSAIDs due to potential increased bleeding risk with thrombocytopenia
output.formulary_status: Standard formulary, prior authorization not required for approved indications
alternatives: Lomustine (CCNU) — alternative alkylating agent if temozolomide is not tolerated
Bevacizumab — may be added at recurrence, not standard in upfront setting
Clinical trial enrollment should be discussed

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4, left temporal lobe

differential.key_distinguishing: Typically shows homogeneous enhancement, periventricular location, and restricted diffusion; immunocompetent patient with irregular enhancement pattern makes this less likely. Steroids may cause diagnostic confusion if given before biopsy.

Solitary metastasis is possible but infiltrative borders are atypical for metastasis. Normal CT chest/abdomen/pelvis argues against an occult primary. Metastases tend to be more spherical at the gray-white junction.

Can mimic a brain tumor in middle-aged women, but typically shows open ring enhancement, less mass effect relative to size, and may have prior or concurrent MS history. MR spectroscopy and perfusion patterns differ from high-grade glioma.

The presence of enhancement, elevated rCBV on perfusion, and high choline on spectroscopy argue against a predominantly low-grade lesion, though mixed-grade components are possible. Biopsy is definitive.

Ring-enhancing lesion with restricted diffusion centrally (pus) and smooth thin wall. Patient is afebrile with normal WBC and no infectious risk factors. Spectroscopy would show amino acid peaks rather than elevated choline.

optimal_actions.action: Perform thorough neurological examination with attention to language function, visual fields, and cognitive status
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle word-finding difficulty (anomia), questionable right upper quadrant visual field cut, mild right facial asymmetry — findings localizing to the left temporal lobe

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast including DWI sequences
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Irregular heterogeneously enhancing left temporal mass with surrounding edema and mild mass effect, not classic ring enhancement pattern

optimal_actions.action: Obtain baseline labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, hepatic panel, and TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal baseline labs establishing candidacy for surgery and chemotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizure activity given the brief staring episodes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal slowing without definite epileptiform discharges; supports structural lesion localization

optimal_actions.action: Order MR perfusion to further characterize the lesion grade
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV (ratio 3.8) in enhancing component, supporting high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Order MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks — classic high-grade glioma spectroscopic pattern

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis to rule out an occult primary malignancy (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of systemic malignancy, supporting primary CNS tumor

optimal_actions.action: Obtain functional MRI for pre-surgical language and motor mapping given left temporal location
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left hemisphere language dominance; Wernicke's area in close proximity (1.2 cm) to tumor margin; guides surgical approach

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anti-seizure medication prophylaxis (levetiracetam 500 mg BID) given location and staring episodes, and start dexamethasone 4 mg every 6 hours for perilesional edema
Refer to neurosurgery for stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection with awake craniotomy and intraoperative language mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Histopathology confirms glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, MGMT promoter methylated

optimal_actions.action: Refer to neuro-oncology for initiation of Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide and radiation, followed by adjuvant temozolomide) with consideration of tumor treating fields
Conduct multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and address goals of care with patient and family

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the lesion — CT alone is insufficient for surgical planning and tumor characterization
Perform metastatic workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) before assuming the lesion is a primary CNS tumor
Obtain tissue diagnosis via biopsy or resection — do not treat empirically based on imaging alone
Obtain functional MRI and/or intraoperative language mapping before surgical resection given proximity to Wernicke's area in the dominant hemisphere
Request molecular testing including IDH status, MGMT promoter methylation, and 1p/19q codeletion on biopsy tissue — these guide treatment and prognosis
Initiate anti-seizure prophylaxis given the left temporal location and clinical concern for subclinical seizures (staring episodes)
contraindicated_actions: Initiating high-dose steroids before obtaining tissue diagnosis if lymphoma is in the differential — steroids can cause lymphoma to regress, rendering biopsy non-diagnostic
Performing aggressive surgical resection without pre-operative language mapping given the proximity to Wernicke's area — risk of permanent aphasia
Discharging the patient without addressing the staring episodes and seizure risk
Starting empiric chemotherapy or radiation without tissue confirmation and molecular profiling
Performing lumbar puncture in the presence of a mass lesion with mass effect without appropriate precautions — risk of herniation
key_reasoning_points: The atypical enhancement pattern (heterogeneous without classic ring enhancement) makes this a moderate-difficulty case — classic GBM presents with ring enhancement and central necrosis, but this lesion shows irregular enhancement raising a broader differential
Word-finding difficulty in a language teacher is a highly meaningful symptom that localizes to the dominant temporal lobe and should not be dismissed as anxiety or depression
The brief staring episodes described by the family may represent focal seizures (temporal lobe) and warrant EEG evaluation and anti-seizure medication
Left temporal location adjacent to Wernicke's area creates a surgical dilemma — maximal resection improves survival but aggressive resection risks permanent receptive aphasia
MGMT promoter methylation is the most important molecular prognostic marker for temozolomide response; its presence in this case is a favorable finding
IDH-wildtype status with TERT promoter mutation and EGFR amplification confirms the molecular diagnosis of GBM even though the imaging is atypical
The personality change (apathy, emotional flattening) may reflect tumor infiltration into the insular cortex and temporal limbic structures, not depression
MR perfusion and spectroscopy provide non-invasive methods to raise diagnostic confidence pre-operatively, but tissue diagnosis remains essential

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate - 52-year-old woman with 2-month personality change and word-finding difficulty. MRI shows an irregular left temporal enhancing lesion without classic ring enhancement or necrosis, broadening the differential. Proximity to language cortex adds surgical complexity.
clinical_pearls: Not all glioblastomas present with the classic ring-enhancing pattern — irregular heterogeneous enhancement can also represent high-grade glioma, especially when perfusion and spectroscopy show aggressive features
Progressive word-finding difficulty in a previously articulate individual is a red flag for dominant temporal lobe pathology and should prompt urgent imaging
MGMT promoter methylation status is the strongest predictor of benefit from temozolomide chemotherapy in glioblastoma and should always be obtained
Functional MRI and intraoperative mapping are essential when tumors are near eloquent cortex to balance maximal resection (which improves survival) against functional preservation
